purpose: Craft examples for three-act film structure, sequence turns, visual motifs, character wound, cost, and payoff-driven dramatic writing.
source_priority_rules: Theme question, character wound, key motifs, sequences, and payoff objects must come from source or locked payload.
Do not add prestige-film symbolism or backstory not supported by source.
Use film scale through sequence logic and visual consequence, not empty slow pacing.
genre_boundary: Track act logic, sequence turns, escalating cost, and visual payoff setup.
Prefer visual storytelling and subtext over explanatory narration when the source allows it.
Every major reversal should change goal, cost, or self-understanding.

scene_card_examples.visual: A sequence-end image pays off an earlier source-grounded object or motif and changes how the protagonist reads the central problem.
scene_card_examples.voiceover: The image does not just end the scene; it changes the meaning of what came before it.

asset_goal.purpose: motif payoff, sequence turn, character isolation or decision

script_scene_examples.visual: The protagonist makes a visible choice that protects one value and sacrifices another.
script_scene_examples.voiceover: The choice matters because the cost is visible, not because the dialogue explains it.

dialogue.speaker: protagonist
dialogue.line: If I keep this, I lose the rest.

script_scene_examples.action_result: A sequence turn is locked by cost, not just mood.

dialogue_style_examples: Film dialogue should reveal choice, wound, value, or cost with restraint.
Avoid short-drama hook stacking when the beat should carry sequence weight.

negative_examples.bad: A scene explains its own symbolism out loud.
negative_examples.why_bad: That weakens visual storytelling and undercuts film rhythm.

forbidden_drift: Do not add unsupported theme statements, prestige symbols, or childhood trauma.
Do not treat every beat like a vertical short-drama cliffhanger.
Do not create payoff images with no setup in source or locked payload.

image: Lock motif objects, wide-frame composition intent, and sequence-ending imagery.
video: Design actions and transitions around sequence logic, not isolated short-form spikes.
